skill.name: convex-create-component
skill.description: Designs and builds Convex components with isolated tables, clear boundaries, and app-facing wrappers. Use this skill when creating a new Convex component, extracting reusable backend logic into a component, building a third-party integration that owns its own tables, packaging Convex functionality for reuse, or when the user mentions defineComponent, app.use, ComponentApi, ctx.runQuery/runMutation across component boundaries, or wants to separate concerns into isolated Convex modules.
skill.summary: Create reusable Convex components with isolated tables, clear boundaries, and secure app-facing wrappers.
skill.author: get-convex

security_audit.summary: All 141 static findings are false positives. The scanner misinterpreted documentation patterns: backticks are markdown code delimiters, 'convex' was flagged as weak crypto (it's a framework name), and env var access references are documentation examples showing proper security patterns. No actual security vulnerabilities exist.

content.value_statement: This skill helps developers design and build Convex components with isolated tables, clear boundaries, and secure app-facing wrappers. It guides users through the component creation workflow with built-in validation and best practices.
actual_capabilities: Design Convex components with isolated tables and clear boundaries
Generate component structure with convex.config.ts, schema.ts, and function files
Create secure app-facing wrappers with authentication and validation
Implement cross-component communication using ctx.runQuery, ctx.runMutation, and ctx.runAction
Follow Convex component best practices for authentication and environment access
Build local, packaged, or hybrid components based on use case
limitations: Does not create the initial Convex project setup
Requires Convex CLI and existing Convex deployment for validation
Cannot access parent app authentication or environment variables directly

use_cases.title: Backend developer building reusable Convex modules
use_cases.description: Extract reusable backend logic into isolated Convex components that can be shared across multiple applications with clear API boundaries.

use_cases.title: Full-stack developer creating third-party integrations
use_cases.description: Build integrations that own their own tables and workflows, keeping third-party logic isolated from the main application codebase.

use_cases.title: Team lead establishing component library patterns
use_cases.description: Define standards for component architecture, authentication boundaries, and app-facing wrappers across the team.

prompt_templates.title: Create a new component
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Convex component for user notifications. The component should own its own notifications table and expose send and list functions.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor existing logic into a component
prompt_templates.prompt: I have payment processing logic scattered across my main convex/ directory. Help me extract it into a reusable component with its own schema and clear boundaries.
prompt_templates.scenario: Extracting existing code into a component

prompt_templates.title: Build a cross-component workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow component that orchestrates between our auth component and notifications component. Show how to pass data across component boundaries securely.

prompt_templates.title: Package a component for distribution
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me package our analytics component as an npm package that can be installed in other Convex projects. Include the proper export structure and documentation patterns.

output_examples.input: Help me create a Convex component for managing user subscriptions
output_examples.output:
I will help you create a subscription component. First, let me confirm a few questions:

1. Should this component be local to your app or packaged for sharing?
2. What tables does the component need to own?
3. Should client access go through app wrappers with auth?

output_examples.input: How do I pass authentication across the component boundary?
output_examples.output:
Keep authentication in the app layer. The component cannot access ctx.auth directly. Pattern:

1. Resolve auth in app mutation/query
2. Pass userId as string argument
3. Use v.string() validator at boundary, not v.id()

best_practices: Keep authentication and environment access in the app layer, not the component
Pass parent app IDs across the boundary as strings since Id types become plain strings at the component boundary
Always add args and returns validators to public component functions for type safety
anti_patterns: Do not expose component functions directly to clients without app wrappers
Do not use v.id() validators for app-owned tables inside component schema
Do not import from the app's _generated files inside components; use the component's own _generated

faq.question: What is the difference between a local and packaged component?
faq.answer: Local components live under convex/components/ and are part of your app. Packaged components can be published as npm packages for use across multiple apps.

faq.question: Why can't components access ctx.auth directly?
faq.answer: Components are isolated and cannot access the parent app's authentication. The app must resolve auth first, then pass userId or identity as explicit arguments.

faq.question: How do I share data between components?
faq.answer: Use ctx.runQuery and ctx.runMutation with components.<name>.<module> syntax. Pass IDs as strings at boundaries and validate them properly.

faq.question: Can components have their own HTTP routes?
faq.answer: No, components cannot register HTTP routes. Mount routes in the app's convex/http.ts and have them call component functions.

faq.question: What happens if I try to use pagination across components?
faq.answer: Standard .paginate() does not work across the component boundary. Use paginator from convex-helpers instead for cross-component pagination.

faq.question: How do I validate data at the component boundary?
faq.answer: Use validators for all public functions with explicit args and returns. Derive validators from your schema when possible to avoid repetition.
